Title: Write a Donor Appeal Built Around One Person, Not the Whole Problem
Teaser: Turns your program facts into an emotionally honest fundraising appeal anchored on a single beneficiary, with a clear ask and a concrete picture of impact.
PromptHint: Paste your cause, who you help, and what the gift funds into [BRIEF]. Add a real story or detail if you have one, plus the gift amounts you want to suggest.
Prompt:
You are a direct-response fundraiser who knows donors give to one person they can picture, not to a statistic about thousands. Here is what I'm raising for:
<brief>
[BRIEF]
</brief>
Who I'm writing to (donor segment): [AUDIENCE]
The specific ask / gift amounts: [ASK]
Think first, silently: choose the single person or moment that makes this cause real, decide the one emotion that should carry the letter, and define exactly what a gift does in concrete terms (what $X buys, fixes, or prevents). Note where my input is thin so I don't invent facts.
Then write the appeal: (1) An opening that drops the reader straight into one person's situation, in scene, not summary. (2) The stakes — what's at risk and why now. (3) The bridge — how the reader's gift changes this specific story. (4) A clear, single ask with 2-3 suggested amounts, each tied to a tangible result ('$50 provides…'). (5) A short, sincere thank-you that assumes generosity. (6) A P.S. that restates the ask and the deadline or urgency.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only stories and facts I gave you — never invent a beneficiary, quote, or number; if I gave none, write '[INSERT real story/detail here]' rather than fabricating. No guilt-tripping or pity language; dignity for the people served. No hype or false urgency. Plain, warm human language.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A complete letter — subject/teaser line, salutation, the six elements as flowing paragraphs (not numbered), suggested-amount lines as a short list, sign-off, and a P.S. Under 400 words unless I say otherwise.

Title: Draft a Grant Proposal Narrative That Answers the Funder's Real Question
Teaser: Builds a structured grant narrative around the problem, your solution, measurable outcomes, and budget logic — written to convince a skeptical program officer, using only facts you supply.
PromptHint: Paste your organization, the program you're seeking funding for, and the outcomes you track into [PROGRAM]. Add the funder's name, priorities, and the amount requested.
Prompt:
You are a grant writer with a high win rate because you write for the program officer's evaluation rubric, not for yourself. Here is the program:
<program>
[PROGRAM]
</program>
Funder and their stated priorities: [FUNDER]
Amount requested and grant period: [REQUEST]
Think first, silently: identify what this funder is actually trying to buy with their grant, where my program aligns with their priorities, and the one outcome that proves impact. Find the gaps a reviewer would poke at — vague metrics, no evidence of need, unclear sustainability — and flag them rather than paper over them.
Then draft the narrative with these sections: (1) Statement of Need — the problem in this community, grounded in the facts/data I gave. (2) Project Description — what we'll do, for whom, over what timeline. (3) Goals & Measurable Outcomes — specific, with how each is measured (mark where I haven't given a metric as '[METRIC NEEDED]'). (4) Organizational Capacity — why we can deliver, using only what I provided. (5) Evaluation — how we'll know it worked. (6) Sustainability — what happens after this grant. (7) Budget Narrative — how the requested amount maps to the work.
CONSTRAINTS: This is a draft for your review, not a guarantee of funding. Never invent statistics, partner names, past results, or credentials — flag every missing fact in brackets. Mirror the funder's language where it's honest to do so. Tight, evidence-led prose; no mission-statement filler.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven sections as labeled headings; outcomes as a table (Goal | Outcome | How measured | Target); a bracketed 'Facts still needed from you' checklist at the end.

Title: Write an Impact Report That Shows Donors Where Their Money Went
Teaser: Turns a year of program activity into a donor-facing impact report that proves results, tells one human story, and makes givers feel like co-owners of the outcome.
PromptHint: Dump your year's numbers, programs, milestones, and any beneficiary stories into [RESULTS]. Note your audience (major donors, small donors, board, public).
Prompt:
You are a nonprofit communications director who writes impact reports donors actually finish, because every number is paired with a face and a 'because of you.' Here's the raw material:
<results>
[RESULTS]
</results>
Who this report is for: [AUDIENCE]
Reporting period: [PERIOD]
Think first, silently: pick the 3-5 numbers that best prove impact (not vanity metrics), choose the one story that makes the year tangible, and decide the single headline takeaway a donor should remember. Separate outputs (what we did) from outcomes (what changed) and lead with outcomes.
Then write the report: (1) A headline and one-line summary of the year's impact. (2) 'By the numbers' — the chosen metrics, each phrased as a result and credited to donors ('Because of you…'). (3) One beneficiary story told in scene (use mine; if absent, mark '[INSERT story]'). (4) 'What changed' — outcomes, honestly stated, including what's still hard. (5) 'What's next' — where you're heading and how donors stay part of it. (6) A genuine thank-you. Keep it skimmable and emotionally warm without overclaiming.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the numbers and stories I provided — never inflate or invent a statistic, and distinguish hard data from estimates. Don't hide setbacks behind cheerful language; a little honesty builds more trust than a glossy spin. No jargon ('leveraged synergies' etc.). Credit donors, not the organization's heroism.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A structured report with the six sections as headings, the metrics as a visual-friendly stat block (Number | What it means), the story as a pull-out, and a closing thank-you line.

Title: Build a Volunteer Recruitment Message That Fills Real Roles
Teaser: Converts a vague 'we need volunteers' into a specific, role-based recruitment message that tells people exactly what they'll do, why it matters, and how easy it is to start.
PromptHint: Describe the volunteer role(s), the time commitment, and who you're trying to reach in [ROLE]. Note where you'll post this (email, social, community board).
Prompt:
You are a volunteer engagement lead who knows people don't sign up for 'help us' — they sign up for a clear job they can picture themselves doing well. Here's what I need filled:
<role>
[ROLE]
</role>
Who I want to reach: [AUDIENCE]
Where this will be posted: [CHANNEL]
Think first, silently: identify the specific person who'd be great at this role and what would make them say yes, the friction that makes people hesitate (time, skill doubt, awkwardness of starting), and the smallest first step that lowers the barrier. Decide the one benefit to the volunteer, not just to you.
Then write the recruitment message: (1) A hook that names the role and the kind of person who'd love it. (2) 'What you'll actually do' — concrete tasks, not abstractions. (3) The commitment — honest time, frequency, and duration. (4) Why it matters — the real-world result their effort creates. (5) 'No experience needed' reassurance or required skills, stated plainly. (6) A dead-simple call to action (one click, one reply, one form). Adapt length and tone to the channel I named.
CONSTRAINTS: Be honest about the time commitment — never undersell it to get sign-ups, which kills retention. Don't promise an experience you can't deliver. Make starting feel easy and low-risk. Warm and specific, never desperate.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-post message sized for the named channel, with the call to action clearly separated, plus 2 alternative hook lines to A/B test.

Title: Plan a Fundraising Campaign From Goal to Calendar
Teaser: Reverse-engineers a fundraising target into a realistic campaign plan: audience segments, asks, channel mix, message themes, and a week-by-week timeline.
PromptHint: Describe your goal amount, deadline, cause, and the donor base you have in [CAMPAIGN]. Note your team size and channels (email, social, events, mail).
Prompt:
You are a development director who builds campaigns backward from the dollar goal, because hope is not a plan. Here's the campaign:
<campaign>
[CAMPAIGN]
</campaign>
My donor base and channels: [ASSETS]
Team capacity: [TEAM]
Think first, silently: estimate where the money realistically comes from (a few major gifts vs. many small ones — apply the rough 'a handful of large gifts carry most of the total' pattern), identify which segments to ask and how, and decide the campaign's single unifying theme. Be honest about whether the goal fits the base and timeline.
Then build the plan: (1) Goal breakdown — a gift-range table showing how many gifts at what level reach the target (label as an estimate to test, not a promise). (2) Audience segments — major donors, lapsed donors, recurring givers, new prospects — and the right ask and channel for each. (3) Campaign theme and core message in one or two sentences. (4) Channel plan — what goes out where and why. (5) A week-by-week timeline from kickoff to close, including a mid-campaign push and a final-48-hours surge. (6) The 3-5 metrics to watch and when to course-correct.
CONSTRAINTS: This is a plan to pressure-test, not a forecast — don't present estimates as certainties. Don't assume tools, staff, or a donor base I didn't describe; flag if the goal looks unrealistic for the inputs. Keep tasks assignable to the team size given. No invented donor data.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A gift-range table (Gift level | # needed | Subtotal | Cumulative), a segment table (Segment | Ask | Channel | Message angle), a week-by-week timeline, and a 'metrics & triggers' list.

Title: Write a Thank-You Sequence That Turns First-Time Donors Into Repeat Givers
Teaser: Designs a multi-message post-gift thank-you and stewardship sequence that makes a new donor feel seen, shows impact, and earns the second gift without asking too soon.
PromptHint: Describe your cause, what a typical first gift funds, and how you can contact donors in [CONTEXT]. Note the gift size or donor type if it matters.
Prompt:
You are a donor retention specialist who knows the second gift is won in the 90 days after the first, by gratitude and proof — not by another ask. Here's my context:
<context>
[CONTEXT]
</context>
What a first gift typically funds: [IMPACT]
Donor type / gift size: [DONOR]
Think first, silently: map the emotional journey of a new donor (did this matter? was I noticed? did it work?), decide what 'impact proof' you can honestly show and when, and choose the moment it's appropriate to invite a deeper relationship rather than push a quick re-ask.
Then design the sequence as 4-5 timed touches: (1) Immediate thank-you (within 48h) — warm, specific, no ask, confirming exactly what their gift does. (2) ~Day 7 — a short story or detail that makes the impact real. (3) ~Day 21 — 'here's what's happening because of you' update. (4) ~Day 45-60 — a soft invitation to go deeper (monthly giving, volunteering, or a survey), framed as belonging, not obligation. (5) Optional milestone touch. For each, give the goal, the channel, a subject line, and the full message.
CONSTRAINTS: No ask in the first two messages — gratitude must come before any request. Use only impact I can truthfully claim. Make each message feel personal, not automated boilerplate. Never imply the relationship is only about money.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A sequence table (Touch | Timing | Goal | Channel | Subject line), followed by the full written copy for each message.

Title: Compose a Board Update That Keeps Directors Informed and Confident
Teaser: Compresses your messy quarter into a clear board update: progress against goals, finances at a glance, risks flagged honestly, and the decisions you actually need from the board.
PromptHint: Dump the quarter's program results, fundraising numbers, financial position, challenges, and any decisions needed into [UPDATES]. Name the period.
Prompt:
You are an executive director who writes board updates that build trust through candor, so directors govern instead of micromanaging. Raw material from the quarter:
<updates>
[UPDATES]
</updates>
Reporting period: [PERIOD]
What I need from the board this cycle (optional): [ASKS]
Think first, silently: decide the single overall health signal for the period (On track / Watch / Concern), the 2-3 things the board most needs to know, and which items genuinely require a board decision versus an FYI. Separate fundraising health from program health from financial health.
Then write the update: (1) Executive summary — the period in 4-5 sentences with an honest health signal. (2) Progress against goals — where we are vs. plan, with brief reasons for any gaps. (3) Fundraising snapshot — raised vs. target, pipeline, notable wins or risks. (4) Financials at a glance — runway/reserves and any flags, in plain terms. (5) Risks & challenges — named honestly, each with what's being done. (6) Decisions needed — specific, time-bound asks with options and your recommendation. (7) Wins worth celebrating. Keep it readable in under 5 minutes.
CONSTRAINTS: This is a governance update, not financial or legal advice — recommend the treasurer/auditor confirm any figures before they're relied on. Never bury bad news under good — surface risks plainly. Don't invent numbers I didn't give; mark gaps '[figure needed]'. Concise; respect busy volunteers' time.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A board memo — health-signal line, the seven labeled sections, the fundraising and financial snapshots as small tables, and a clearly separated 'Decisions requested' block with recommendations.

Title: Make a Major-Gift Ask Script for a Face-to-Face Conversation
Teaser: Prepares you for a high-stakes major-donor conversation: how to open, the moment to make the ask, the specific number, and how to handle hesitation without flinching.
PromptHint: Describe the prospective major donor, their connection to your cause, and what you're raising for in [PROSPECT]. Note the amount you intend to ask for if you've decided.
Prompt:
You are a major-gifts officer who closes transformational gifts because you ask for a specific number, then stay silent and let the donor respond. Here's the situation:
<prospect>
[PROSPECT]
</prospect>
What I'm raising for and why now: [CAUSE]
Intended ask amount (or 'help me decide'): [AMOUNT]
Think first, silently: identify what this donor cares about and how this gift connects to their values, the right moment in the conversation to transition to the ask, and the specific dollar number to name (if I asked for help, suggest a range with reasoning based on what I told you). Anticipate their two or three most likely objections.
Then prepare me: (1) Opening — how to begin warmly and reconnect to their 'why', without small-talking forever. (2) The bridge — how to move from relationship to the case, tying the gift to their values. (3) The ask — the exact words to use to name a specific amount for a specific purpose, then the instruction: stop talking and wait. (4) Handling responses — scripts for 'I need to think', 'that's too much', 'let me talk to my spouse/advisor', and an enthusiastic yes. (5) The close and clear next step regardless of answer. (6) What NOT to do (over-explain, negotiate against yourself, fill the silence).
CONSTRAINTS: This is conversation coaching, not financial or tax advice for the donor — if tax-deductibility comes up, advise them to consult their own advisor. Name a specific number, never a vague 'whatever you can'. Don't manipulate or pressure; respect a 'no'. Keep the donor's dignity and agency central.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A conversation playbook with the six sections, the ask itself as a verbatim scripted line, and objection responses as 'If they say… → you say…' pairs.

Title: Turn a Boring Event Recap Into a Re-Engagement Email Series
Teaser: Transforms what happened at your fundraiser or event into a short follow-up email series that thanks attendees, reports the result, and converts momentum into gifts or sign-ups.
PromptHint: Describe the event, who attended, what was raised or achieved, and your follow-up goal in [EVENT]. Note any unmet target or next opportunity.
Prompt:
You are an events-and-development lead who treats the week after an event as the real fundraising window, while gratitude and energy are still warm. Here's the event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Who attended / received this: [AUDIENCE]
My follow-up goal: [GOAL]
Think first, silently: capture the emotional high point of the event everyone shared, decide what 'we did this together' result to report, and choose the one next action that converts the moment (final donations to hit a target, a recurring-gift invite, a save-the-date, a volunteer ask). Sequence gratitude before any ask.
Then write a 3-email series: (1) Within 24-48h — pure thank-you and the headline result, no ask, celebrating attendees as the reason. (2) ~Day 4 — a story, photo prompt, or detail that relives the impact, with a gentle bridge toward the goal. (3) ~Day 7-10 — the clear ask or invitation tied to the goal, with urgency only if it's real (e.g., a matching deadline). For each: goal, subject line, full copy.
CONSTRAINTS: First email has no ask. Use only what actually happened and the numbers I gave — no invented totals or quotes. No manufactured urgency; if there's a real deadline or match, use it, otherwise don't fake one. Warm, celebratory, human tone.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A series table (Email | Timing | Goal | Subject line), then the full copy for each email, with the call to action in emails 2-3 visually separated.

Title: Write a Recurring-Giving Pitch That Makes Monthly the Obvious Choice
Teaser: Builds a persuasive case for monthly giving, reframing a small recurring amount as steady, compounding impact and the most powerful thing a supporter can do.
PromptHint: Describe your cause and what reliable monthly funding lets you do that one-off gifts can't in [CONTEXT]. Note the monthly amounts you want to suggest.
Prompt:
You are a fundraiser who knows monthly donors are worth far more over time than one-time givers, and that the pitch must sell predictability and belonging, not just convenience. Here's my context:
<context>
[CONTEXT]
</context>
Who I'm asking: [AUDIENCE]
Suggested monthly amounts: [AMOUNTS]
Think first, silently: identify what reliable, predictable funding specifically unlocks that lump sums can't (planning, year-round programs, reaching people in lean months), and frame a modest monthly figure as a concrete recurring result. Decide the identity you're inviting the donor into ('you become the person who…').
Then write the pitch: (1) A hook that reframes 'a small amount monthly' as quietly powerful. (2) The case — why steady support changes what's possible, in concrete terms. (3) The math made tangible — what each suggested monthly amount does over a year ('$15/month = … by next year'). (4) The belonging angle — what joining the monthly community means and any (real) recognition or perks. (5) Reassurance — easy to start, easy to change or cancel, full control. (6) A simple, low-friction call to action with the suggested amounts. 
CONSTRAINTS: Don't overstate what a single donor's monthly gift achieves — keep the impact math honest and use only facts I gave. No dark patterns or making cancellation sound hard. Emphasize donor control and dignity. Warm, confident, never pleading.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A complete pitch (web/email length) with the six elements, the impact math as a small 'Your monthly gift / Over a year' table, and the call to action with suggested amounts clearly presented.

Title: Draft a Grant Report That Wins the Next Grant
Teaser: Turns how you spent a grant into a funder report that demonstrates results, handles what didn't go to plan with credibility, and quietly sets up renewed funding.
PromptHint: Paste the funded project, what the grant paid for, the results, and any deviations from the original plan into [RESULTS]. Name the funder and the grant terms.
Prompt:
You are a grants manager who treats every grant report as the first step of the renewal, because funders re-fund organizations that deliver and tell the truth. Here's what happened:
<results>
[RESULTS]
</results>
Funder and what was promised in the original grant: [GRANT]
Reporting period: [PERIOD]
Think first, silently: match what we promised against what we delivered, decide how to present outcomes the funder will care about, and figure out how to handle any shortfall or change of plan with credibility rather than excuses. Distinguish what we did (outputs) from what changed (outcomes).
Then write the report: (1) Summary — what the grant funded and the headline result. (2) Outcomes vs. goals — a clear comparison of promised vs. achieved, with honest notes on any gaps and what was learned. (3) The story — one human example of the impact (use mine; mark '[INSERT]' if none). (4) Use of funds — how the money mapped to the work, in plain terms. (5) Challenges & adaptations — what didn't go as planned and how you responded (this builds trust, not doubt). (6) What's next — the continued need and, if appropriate, a soft bridge to future partnership. 
CONSTRAINTS: This is a draft to verify against your records — confirm all figures before submitting. Never inflate results or hide a missed target; a credibly explained shortfall beats a suspicious perfect score. Use only the data I gave; flag missing numbers in brackets. Professional, candid tone matched to the funder.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The six sections as headings, an outcomes table (Goal promised | Achieved | Notes), a use-of-funds summary, and a bracketed 'Verify before sending' checklist.

Title: Write a Lapsed-Donor Win-Back Letter That Reopens the Door
Teaser: Crafts a warm, non-accusatory message to donors who've gone quiet — acknowledging the gap, showing what's changed, and inviting them back without guilt.
PromptHint: Describe who lapsed (how long ago, past giving level) and what's new or improved at your organization since they left in [CONTEXT]. Note the action you want them to take.
Prompt:
You are a donor relations specialist who wins back lapsed supporters by treating them like missed friends, not delinquent accounts. Here's the situation:
<context>
[CONTEXT]
</context>
Who I'm writing to (how long lapsed, past giving): [SEGMENT]
What I want them to do: [ACTION]
Think first, silently: figure out why donors usually drift (they felt unseen, forgot, or never saw impact — rarely because they stopped caring), decide what's genuinely new or proven since they left that's worth coming back for, and choose a re-entry ask small enough to feel easy. Lead with warmth, not a bigger ask than they ever gave.
Then write the win-back letter: (1) A warm opening that acknowledges time has passed, without scolding ('We've missed you' energy, not 'you stopped giving'). (2) A brief 'here's what your past support helped build' — crediting them for earlier impact. (3) What's new or stronger now — a concrete reason to re-engage. (4) An honest, gentle invitation back with a low-friction first step (the named action). (5) A sincere close that leaves the door open even if they're not ready. (6) A P.S. that restates the easy next step.
CONSTRAINTS: No guilt, no 'we noticed you haven't given', no pressure. Use only true facts about your organization's progress. Make returning feel welcoming and low-stakes. Keep the ask modest — reconnection first, generosity follows. Warm, sincere, human.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A complete letter — subject/teaser line, salutation, the six elements as natural paragraphs, sign-off, and a P.S. Under 300 words unless I say otherwise.
